feedback.text: I am in support of this act but would like for it's fairness to extend to digital products as well. No one should be able to take a fridge you bought 10 years ago from you, nor a movie, nor a videogame. I had this happen to me with the Blizzard game Overwatch. I bough the game the year it came out in 2016. I knew exactly what I was buying which was a 6 versus 6 online shooter with all classes equally viable. When Overwatch 2 came out with its marketing full of deceptivities in 2023 I lost access to that product. My personal account was transfered to Overwatch 2 but that was not same game anymore. In short it changed the balance of the game to favor tank class which would heavily influence my purchase decision since I do not like such balance. It had other unwanted changes that would influence my buying decision but I want to keep this short. With the recent destroying of the game The Crew released in 2014 with no refunds I am worried about the game that I purchased from the same developer Ubisoft. The Division was released in 2016 and it could also be coming to a closure. I am not an active player but I do jump in from time to time to enjoy the game. This product that I paid for can be taken from me legally because it was designed as such with no disclosure of its lifetime. If I knew that this game could be taken down, I would have never bough it in the first place. I think the Stop Destroying Videogames initiative which has collected almost 1,5 million signatures confidently describes the magnitude of this problem. Developers, or rather people in charge need to stop designing the videogames in such a way that they do not function after the developers decide to stop supporting them. They have every right to stop supporting products that no longer bring them enough revenue to justify their support, but customers should also have every right to keep the products they purchased. If a model of vacuum cleaner stops being produced it does not magically stop working, neither should games.
